title: Crawl Graph, Frontier State Machine, Scheduling Identity, and Invariants · Changed-Schema Lab
summary: An independent municipal service owner supplies a different origin, markup family, dynamic boundary, accessibility need, resource budget, and injected failure and transfers crawl graph, frontier state machine, scheduling identity, and invariants into a changed deterministic frontier transition model. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of deterministic frontier transition model with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theo…
objectives: Define discovered, rejected, queued, claimed, fetching, fetched, parsed, failed, retryable, terminal, and canceled transitions.
Atomically reserve admitted URL identity when scheduling so concurrent discovery cannot duplicate effects.
Prioritize owner commitments, depth, page classes, freshness, origin fairness, and retries with stable tie rules.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Lab Frontier States
steps.instruction:
lab case — A lab TypeScript team handles case 1431 during an isolated browser, package, and restore smoke. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove legal frontier states, reservation timing, stable priorities, graph evidence, and invariants; every Promise, stream, listener, timer, socket, file, browser context, and report sink needs one cleanup owner; then change one test, mutation, package, browser binary, container, capacity, incident, restore, rollback, or residual-risk condition and reconcile release evidence. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Lab Frontier States.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for define discovered, rejected, queued, claimed, fetching, fetched, parsed, failed, retryable, terminal, and canceled transitions. For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Lab Frontier States, keep Frontier States connected to executable TypeScript authorization, URL, runtime admission, Fetch, Promise, browser, accessibility, report, package, and recovery decision evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab State Invariants
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-TypeScript function headed "// Evidence: crawler-ts-state-invariants-lsun3x" that uses case 4848 to prove legal frontier states, reservation timing, stable priorities, graph evidence, and invariants. Keep defaults runnable and return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not import Node APIs, open streams or sockets, resolve DNS, parse through Cheerio, launch Playwright or axe-core, access files or packages, retain credentials, leave handles, or claim native runtime, accessibility, privacy, legal, load, container, restore, or production behavior; verify those boundaries later with Node 24.18.0, TypeScript 7.0.2 and the TypeScript 6.0.2 compatibility compiler API, Cheerio 1.2.0, robots-parser 3.0.1, p-limit 7.3.0, csv-stringify 6.8.1, Playwright 1.61.1, axe-core 4.12.1, original fixtures, loopback servers, emitted-runtime and handle tests, controlled DNS and browser environments, accessibility review, packages, containers, capacity, backup, restore, rollback, legal review, and release gates.
